workflow.overview: Step-by-step implementation workflow for Carbon components, including pre-integration analysis, grid system usage, component integration, and tool usage patterns.

pre_integration_analysis.title: Pre-Integration Analysis
pre_integration_analysis.description: Before implementing any new feature or component, conduct thorough analysis of existing patterns to ensure consistency and avoid unnecessary changes.

guidance.point: Use search_files to find similar functionality or component usage across the codebase
Use list_code_definition_names to understand the project's architectural patterns
Use read_file to examine how other pages/features implement comparable UI elements
Identify which component libraries are already in use and their usage patterns

step.rationale: Understanding existing patterns prevents introducing inconsistent approaches and ensures new implementations align with established project conventions.
step.example:
// Before adding a new form component, search for existing form patterns <search_files> <path>src</path> <regex>TextInput|Form|validation</regex> <file_pattern>*.jsx</file_pattern> </search_files>
// Examine existing form implementations <read_file> <args> <file> <path>src/components/UserForm.jsx</path> </file> </args> </read_file>

step.action: Read local component source before using

guidance.point: MANDATORY: Use read_file to read the source code of ANY local component before using it for the first time
Understand the component's props, especially render props or special integration points
Check for patterns like renderPageHeader, actionBarItems, or similar that indicate how to add content
Document available props and their purposes in your analysis
Never assume how a component works - always verify by reading its implementation

step.rationale: Reading component source prevents misuse and ensures you leverage all available features rather than creating unnecessary custom implementations.

enforcement.rule: ALWAYS use read_file to examine local component source code before first use
Document all available props and their purposes in your analysis
Never assume component behavior - always verify by reading the source
If a component has specialized props (like renderPageHeader), you MUST use them

guidance.point: Use read_file to examine build configuration files (vite.config.js, webpack.config.js, etc.)
Check for SSR indicators if adding packages with potential browser-only code
Verify package compatibility with the project's build system
See working_practices.xml for SSR awareness guidance
Use the simplest approach that matches existing project patterns
Avoid introducing new component libraries or patterns unless necessary
Prefer extending existing patterns over creating new ones

step.key_question: "How does this project already solve similar problems?" - Always use tools to check existing implementations before introducing new patterns.

grid_system.title: Carbon Grid System Usage
grid_system.description: Comprehensive guidance for using the Carbon Grid system for responsive layouts. The Grid system is fundamental to all Carbon implementations.

mandatory_requirements.requirement: ALWAYS use Carbon Grid for all page layouts
Configure responsive column widths for ALL breakpoints (sm, md, lg)
Create SEPARATE Grid components for each distinct logical content group

carbon_breakpoints.title: Carbon Breakpoints
carbon_breakpoints.description: Carbon uses a responsive grid system with five breakpoints.

grid_variants.title: Grid Variants
grid_variants.description: Carbon provides three grid variants with different horizontal gaps.

variant.name: default
variant.usage: Use for most layouts - provides comfortable spacing
variant.example: <Grid> <Column sm={4} md={8} lg={16}> Content with default 32px gap </Column> </Grid>

variant.name: narrow
variant.usage: Use for denser layouts where space is limited
variant.example: <Grid narrow> <Column sm={4} md={8} lg={16}> Content with narrow 16px gap </Column> </Grid>

variant.name: condensed
variant.usage: Use only for specialized interfaces requiring maximum content density
variant.example: <Grid condensed> <Column sm={4} md={8} lg={16}> Content with no gap </Column> </Grid>

vertical_spacing_when_wrapping.title: Vertical Spacing When Content Wraps
vertical_spacing_when_wrapping.description: When grid content wraps to new rows, vertical spacing should match the horizontal gutter spacing to maintain visual consistency.

spacing_rules.rule: Vertical spacing between wrapped rows = horizontal gutter spacing
Use row-gap on Grid component (Carbon Columns use margins for alignment in CSS Grid)
This applies to all grid variants (default, narrow, condensed)

best_practices.practice: Always use row-gap on the Grid component, not margin-bottom on Columns
Carbon Grid columns use margins for horizontal alignment, so row-gap is the correct property for vertical spacing
Always match vertical spacing (row-gap) to the grid variant's horizontal gutter
Test responsive behavior to ensure spacing remains consistent when content wraps
Document the grid variant used so vertical spacing can be correctly applied

grid_variant_selection.title: Grid Variant Selection Criteria
grid_variant_selection.description: Choose the appropriate grid variant based on content type and density requirements.

criterion.name: narrow
criterion.when_to_use: Content-focused sections with tiles or cards

use_cases.use_case: Dashboard tiles displaying metrics or data

criterion.example: <Grid narrow> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Metric Card 1</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Metric Card 2</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Metric Card 3</Tile> </Column> </Grid>

criterion.name: condensed
criterion.when_to_use: Data-dense areas requiring maximum content density
criterion.example: <Grid condensed> <Column sm={4} md={8} lg={16}> <DataTable rows={rows} headers={headers} /> </Column> </Grid>

criterion.name: default
criterion.when_to_use: General page layouts with comfortable spacing

use_cases.use_case: When comfortable reading and breathing room is desired

criterion.example:
Page Title

Content with comfortable spacing
Sidebar content

selection_guidelines.guideline: Use narrow grid for content-focused sections with tiles or cards
Use condensed grid for data-dense areas like tables and forms
Use default grid for general page layouts with comfortable spacing
Consider the visual weight and density of content when choosing
Maintain consistency within similar content sections

figma_layout_guide_understanding.title: Understanding Figma Layout Guides and Grid Variants
figma_layout_guide_understanding.description: Critical guidance for interpreting Figma Layout guides and identifying the actual grid variant being used in designs.

layout_guide_limitation.title: Figma Layout Guide Limitation

key_points.point: Figma Layout guides typically represent the default Carbon grid (32px gutter)
Narrow and condensed grids are NOT reflected in Figma Layout guides
Don't assume the Layout guide shows the actual grid variant being used

layout_guide_limitation.rationale: Figma's Layout guide feature has limitations and cannot accurately represent narrow (16px gutter) or condensed (0px gutter) grid variants. Designers may use these variants in their designs, but the Layout guide will still show the default 32px gutter spacing.

visual_indicators.title: Visual Indicators for Grid Variants
visual_indicators.description: Use these visual cues to determine the actual grid variant, regardless of what the Figma Layout guide shows.

indicator.title: Narrow Grid Indicators

visual_cues.cue: Content appears shifted left compared to Layout guide
Columns appear slightly wider than Layout guide suggests
Horizontal spacing between elements is tighter (16px instead of 32px)
Content doesn't align with Layout guide column boundaries

indicator.technical_explanation: Narrow grid removes 16px from the left margin of each column, shifting content left and widening each column slightly

indicator.title: Condensed Grid Indicators

visual_cues.cue: Content has no gaps between columns
Elements appear very tight and dense
No visible horizontal spacing between adjacent items
Content appears significantly wider than Layout guide suggests

indicator.technical_explanation: Condensed grid removes 16px from left AND right of each column, resulting in no gaps between columns

visual_cues.cue: Content aligns with Figma Layout guide
Column boundaries match Layout guide exactly

column_width_differences.title: Column Width Differences by Variant
column_width_differences.description: Understanding how column widths differ between grid variants helps identify which variant is being used.

variant.name: default
variant.behavior: Columns align with Figma Layout guide
variant.column_width: Standard width as shown in Layout guide

variant.name: narrow
variant.behavior: Each column is ~16px wider (left margin removed)
variant.visual_effect: Content shifts left, columns appear wider

variant.name: condensed
variant.behavior: Each column is ~32px wider (left and right margins removed)
variant.column_width: Significantly wider than Layout guide shows
variant.visual_effect: No gaps between columns, very tight layout

analysis_approach.title: Step-by-Step Analysis Approach
analysis_approach.description: Follow this systematic approach to determine the actual grid variant being used in a Figma design.

step.action: Check if Figma has a Layout guide
step.method: Use Figma MCP tools to inspect the design
step.note: Layout guide presence doesn't guarantee it shows the correct variant

step.action: Compare actual content positioning to the Layout guide

questions.question: Does content align with Layout guide column boundaries?
Is content shifted left compared to the guide?
Are there visible gaps between columns?

step.action: Use content density and spacing to confirm grid variant

indicators.indicator: Tighter spacing, content shifted left → narrow

example.scenario: Figma shows Layout guide but content is shifted left and columns appear wider than the guide suggests
example.analysis: - Layout guide shows default 32px gutter - Actual content doesn't align with guide - Content appears shifted left - Columns are wider than guide indicates

example.scenario: Content has no gaps between columns and appears very tight
example.analysis: - No visible horizontal spacing between elements - Content appears very dense - Columns are significantly wider than Layout guide - Elements touch or nearly touch each other

example.scenario: Content aligns perfectly with Layout guide
example.analysis: - Content boundaries match Layout guide columns - Comfortable 32px spacing visible - No misalignment or shifting

best_practices.practice: Never assume the Figma Layout guide represents the actual grid variant
Always analyze actual content positioning, not just the Layout guide
Use multiple visual indicators to confirm grid variant selection
Measure horizontal spacing between elements to verify grid type
When in doubt, ask the user or designer which grid variant is intended
Document your grid variant analysis in comments for future reference

mistake.error: Assuming Layout guide always shows the correct grid variant
mistake.consequence: Implementing wrong grid variant, incorrect spacing
mistake.correction: Always verify by analyzing actual content positioning

mistake.error: Not checking for content misalignment with Layout guide
mistake.consequence: Missing narrow or condensed grid usage
mistake.correction: Compare content boundaries to Layout guide systematically

mistake.consequence: Choosing default grid when narrow/condensed is needed
mistake.correction: Assess overall content density and spacing patterns

column_calculation.title: Column Span Calculation
column_calculation.description: Calculate the number of columns an element should span based on its width using a robust, fuzzy determination approach that accounts for gutters and real-world design variations.

priority.description: When Figma explicitly shows column counts or grid overlays, use those values
Calculate based on percentage of total grid width with reasonable rounding
Use formula as a starting point, then verify with gutter considerations

fuzzy_determination_approach.title: Fuzzy Column Determination
fuzzy_determination_approach.description: Instead of strict mathematical ceiling, use percentage-based determination with reasonable rounding to account for design intent and gutter spacing.

step.action: Measure the full width of the content group
step.guidance: Include all visual content, not just element bounds

step.action: Calculate percentage of total grid width
Determine columns based on percentage ranges
Round to nearest reasonable column count
step.guidance: Don't always round up - consider design intent and visual balance. If content is slightly under a column boundary, rounding down may be appropriate.

example.scenario: Content spans approximately 33% of grid width
example.analysis: - Grid width: 1200px (lg, 16 columns) - Content width: ~400px - Percentage: 400/1200 = 33.3% - Likely columns: 4-5 columns (one-third of 16) - Verification: 5 cols × 75px + 4 gutters × 32px = 503px ✓ - Decision: Use 5 columns

example.scenario: Content spans approximately 50% of grid width
example.analysis: - Grid width: 1200px (lg, 16 columns) - Content width: ~600px - Percentage: 600/1200 = 50% - Likely columns: 8 columns (half of 16) - Verification: 8 cols × 75px + 7 gutters × 32px = 824px - Note: Includes gutter space, content fits comfortably - Decision: Use 8 columns

traditional_formula.title: Traditional Formula (Use with Caution)
traditional_formula.formula: Math.ceil((width in pixels) / ((grid width) / (number of columns)))

limitations.limitation: Does not account for gutter spacing
Always rounds up, which may not match design intent
Can result in columns that are too wide

traditional_formula.when_to_use: Use as a starting point, then verify with fuzzy determination approach

guidelines.guideline: Prefer design-specified column counts from Figma when available
Use percentage-based fuzzy determination for calculated columns
Always verify calculations by checking if content + gutters fits within calculated columns
When column settings are calculated for lg, xlg or max, apply the calculated number of columns to lg unless otherwise instructed
Consider rounding to nearest reasonable column count, not always ceiling
Verify column spans add up correctly at each breakpoint
Account for gutter spacing in all calculations

pattern.result: <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={5}>Tile 1</Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={5}>Tile 2</Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={6}>Tile 3</Column>
<Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}>Tile 1</Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}>Tile 2</Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}>Tile 3</Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}>Tile 4</Column>

logical_content_groups.title: MANDATORY: Separate Grids for Logical Content Groups
logical_content_groups.description: EVERY distinct logical content group MUST be placed in its own Grid component. This is NOT optional and must be implemented automatically.

definition.title: What is a Logical Content Group?

criteria.criterion: Content elements that should wrap together responsively
Elements that form a cohesive functional unit (e.g., header, list of tiles, footer)
Content under the same heading or subheading
Elements with the same presentation style that should behave as a unit

logical_content_groups.critical_rule: Different logical content groups (that should NOT wrap together) MUST use separate Grid components. Failure to use separate grids for distinct logical content groups is a critical error that must be avoided in all implementations.

good_example.title: CORRECT: Separate grids for header and content
good_example.code:
{/* Header is its own logical group */} <Grid> <Column lg={16}> <PageHeader title="Dashboard" /> </Column> </Grid>
{/* Tiles are a separate logical group that should wrap together */} <Grid> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 1</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 2</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 3</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 4</Tile> </Column> </Grid>
{/* Footer is its own logical group */} <Grid> <Column lg={16}> <Footer /> </Column> </Grid>

bad_example.title: INCORRECT: Single grid mixing different logical groups
bad_example.code: {/* ❌ WRONG: Header and tiles should NOT be in the same Grid */} <Grid> <Column lg={16}> <PageHeader title="Dashboard" /> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 1</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 2</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 3</Tile> </Column> </Grid>
bad_example.why_wrong: The header and tiles are different logical groups. The header should stay at the top, while tiles should wrap together as a group. Mixing them in one Grid breaks this separation.

nested_grid_pattern.title: Nested Grid Pattern
nested_grid_pattern.description: When a Column needs to contain multiple items that should wrap together, use a nested Grid inside that Column. This is different from using separate Grids for different logical sections.

when_to_use.use_case: A column contains multiple items that need to wrap responsively as a group
Creating a sub-layout within a larger layout structure
Items within a section need their own grid-based positioning

nested_grids.description: Grid within Column - for items that wrap together within a parent column
nested_grids.structure: Outer Grid → Column → Inner Grid → Columns

separate_grids.description: Multiple top-level Grids - for distinct logical sections that should NOT wrap together
separate_grids.structure: Grid → Columns, then separate Grid → Columns

nested_grid_pattern.example:
{/* Outer Grid for main layout */}

Sidebar

Left content

Main Content Area

{/* Nested Grid for tiles that wrap together */} Tile 1 Tile 2 Tile 3 Tile 4

usage_guidelines.guideline: Use nested Grids when a Column needs to contain multiple wrapping items
The nested Grid inherits the parent Column's width constraints
Nested Grids can use different variants (narrow, condensed) than the outer Grid
Calculate nested Grid column spans based on the parent Column's width, not the full page width

scenario.title: Dashboard with sidebar and tile grid
scenario.description: Sidebar in one column, tiles in another column using nested Grid

scenario.title: Content area with multiple card groups
scenario.description: Each card group uses a nested Grid within its parent Column

scenario.title: Form sections with grouped fields
scenario.description: Form sections use nested Grids for field layout within form columns

responsive_implementation.title: Responsive Column Configuration
responsive_implementation.description: ALWAYS specify column spans for sm, md, and lg breakpoints to ensure proper responsive behavior.
responsive_implementation.requirement: Every Column component MUST have sm, md, and lg props defined

pattern.name: Full Width
pattern.description: Element spans full width at all breakpoints

pattern.name: Stacked on Mobile, Side-by-Side on Desktop
pattern.description: Two elements stack on mobile, sit side-by-side on desktop
pattern.code: <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={8}>Left Content</Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={8}>Right Content</Column>

pattern.name: Responsive Tiles
pattern.description: Tiles that adapt to screen size
pattern.code: {/* 1 column on mobile, 2 on tablet, 4 on desktop */} <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 1</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 2</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 3</Tile> </Column> <Column sm={4} md={4} lg={4}> <Tile>Tile 4</Tile> </Column>

practices.practice: Use full-width columns (lg={16}) for headers and footers
Ensure column spans add up correctly at each breakpoint
Test responsive behavior at all breakpoints
Use Grid condensed/narrow variants when appropriate for content density
Keep Grid components focused on single logical content groups
Use consistent column patterns throughout the application

component_integration.title: Component Integration Patterns
component_integration.description: Patterns for integrating Carbon components into the application with targeted, minimal modifications.

targeted_modifications.title: Use Targeted Edits with apply_diff
targeted_modifications.description: Make precise, line-specific changes rather than rewriting entire sections.

principles.principle: Use apply_diff with exact SEARCH/REPLACE blocks for surgical edits
Make multiple related changes in one apply_diff operation
Preserve existing code structure, formatting, and style
Only change what needs to change - nothing more

example.title: Adding a single import
example.code: <apply_diff> <path>src/components/Dashboard.jsx</path> <diff> <<<<<<< SEARCH :start_line:1 ------- import { Grid, Column } from '@carbon/react'; ======= import { Grid, Column, Button } from '@carbon/react'; >>>>>>> REPLACE </diff> </apply_diff>

example.title: Multiple related changes in one apply_diff
example.code:
<apply_diff> <path>src/components/Dashboard.jsx</path> <diff> <<<<<<< SEARCH :start_line:1 ------- import { Grid, Column } from '@carbon/react'; ======= import { Grid, Column, Button } from '@carbon/react'; >>>>>>> REPLACE
<<<<<<< SEARCH :start_line:25 ------- </Column> </Grid> ); ======= </Column> </Grid> <Grid> <Column sm={4} md={8} lg={16}> <Button>New Action</Button> </Column> </Grid> ); >>>>>>> REPLACE </diff> </apply_diff>

principles.principle: Use write_to_file to create new files for new features
Keep feature-specific code in dedicated modules/components
Use clear separation of concerns (separate handler files, style files)
Make additions that can be easily removed if needed

preserving_patterns.title: Preserve Existing Patterns

principles.principle: Use read_file to understand existing code style, indentation, and conventions
Don't introduce new patterns or refactor existing ones
Keep the same import organization and structure

preserving_patterns.key_principle: "Change only what you must, touch only what you need" - Every line changed should have a clear, necessary purpose.

tool_usage_patterns.title: Tool Usage Patterns
tool_usage_patterns.description: Effective use of available tools for understanding context, making informed decisions, and verifying changes.

rules.rule: Always use read_file to understand existing code before modifying it
Never assume file contents - always verify
Read configuration files to understand build system setup
Read component source to understand integration points
Use read_file after apply_diff or write_to_file to confirm changes were applied correctly
Check that syntax is valid and formatting is preserved
Verify that all intended changes were made

tool.name: search_files
tool.when: Locating similar functionality across the codebase

tool.name: list_code_definition_names
tool.when: Getting an overview of available functions and classes

tool.name: apply_diff
tool.when: Making targeted, surgical edits to existing files

tool.name: insert_content
tool.when: Inserting content at specific line numbers

workflow_principle.title: Read, Plan, Execute, Verify
workflow_principle.description: Always use tools to understand before changing, and verify after changing.

steps.step: Read - Use read_file, search_files to understand current state
Execute - Use apply_diff, write_to_file, or insert_content
Verify - Use read_file to confirm changes are correct

implementation_checklist.title: Implementation Checklist
implementation_checklist.description: Use this checklist to ensure all critical steps are completed.

items.item: Used search_files to find similar functionality
Used read_file to examine existing patterns
Used read_file to check build configuration
Used read_file to read local component source code
Identified minimal set of files to modify
Used apply_diff with multiple SEARCH/REPLACE blocks for related changes
Created separate Grid components for each logical content group
Specified column spans for sm, md, and lg breakpoints
Preserved existing code patterns and style
Created new files for new features when appropriate
Used Carbon design tokens for styling
Used read_file to verify all changes were applied correctly
Confirmed all integration points are properly connected
Verified responsive behavior at all breakpoints
